topic.what: Voltage fault injection attacks targeting UAV autopilot failsafe mechanisms to disable safety responses
topic.how: Dual methodology: ARMORY software simulation for vulnerability identification and ChipWhisperer hardware fault injection for exploit validation on STM32 running PX4-Autopilot
topic.why_matters: Bypassing failsafe mechanisms can enable UAV hijacking, mission failure, or catastrophic crashes when safety-critical responses like RTL or Land are suppressed

characteristics.what_it_measures: Percentage of glitch attempts achieving exploitable outcome deviating from expected failsafe behavior
characteristics.evidence: Figure 7,8,11,12,15,17 show success rates varying with glitch offset and width
characteristics.context: Strong temporal clustering observed; early offsets (0-5) and specific windows (21-26, 45-64 cycles) show elevated success rates

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of clock cycles within helper function where fault injection yields exploitable behavior
characteristics.evidence: ARMORY simulations show peaks at these windows across all three scenarios
characteristics.context: RC Signal Loss peaks at 3-4, 24-25, 50-52; Battery Critical at 4-6, 26-27, 66-69; Battery Emergency helper function at 3-7 and 45-64

characteristics.what_itasures: Ratio of faults producing exploitable outcomes relative to total injected faults
characteristics.evidence: Scenario 3 (Battery Emergency) integrated test yielded 1363 exploitable faults from 26976 total injections
characteristics.context: Isolated function tests likely have higher exploit rates; extended glitch duration increases HardFault resets alongside exploits

characteristics.what_it_measures: Voltage glitch pulse duration required for successful fault injection on STM32F407
characteristics.evidence: Figure 8,12,14,17 show varying success across this width range with non-destructive (capacitors retained) configuration
characteristics.context: Longer glitch widths increase both exploitable faults and HardFault-triggered resets; 1.63-1.72 µs appears optimal for most scenarios

characteristics.what_it_measures: Distribution of fault-induced failsafe action outcomes (None, Warning, RTL with error, Invalid State)
characteristics.value: None dominant; RTL metadata error secondary; Invalid State observed in hardware
characteristics.evidence: Figure 9,10,13,14,18,19 show No Action as most frequent outcome in both simulation and hardware
characteristics.context: Battery Emergency scenario: None/Disable most prominent outcome, forcing total inaction during emergency

characteristics.what_it_measures: ARMORY cycle to ChipWhisperer physical offset mapping ratio (168MHz vs 32MHz)
characteristics.evidence: Section V-A establishes clock ratio for temporal alignment between simulation and hardware
characteristics.context: Critical for correlating ARMORY vulnerability windows with hardware glitch offset parameters

good_for: Cyber-Physical System hardware fault injection research
not_for: Non-ARM Cortex-M architectures (different fault model behavior)
Systems without exposed voltage rails (enclosed/custom SoC)
Real-time safety-critical systems requiring uninterrupted operation
requires: STM32F407-Discovery or equivalent ARM Cortex-M4 target
ChipWhisperer-Lite or equivalent voltage glitching platform
ARMORY framework for software fault simulation
Physical access for hardware implant or glitch connection
Reference target for offline profiling and timing characterization

novelty: First comprehensive hardware fault injection analysis on PX4-Autopilot failsafe mechanisms
Dual-method validation correlating ARMORY simulation with ChipWhisperer hardware exploits
Demonstrates timing-sensitive vulnerabilities in safety-critical decision logic
Proposes stealthy physical threat model with miniaturized PCB implant inside flight controller enclosure
Validates that simulated attacks translate to tangible hardware exploitability
